column1: ## Per-run averages (derived from OLS fit + mean token count)
## Important: what the intercept means + system-dependence caveat

column2: The intercept in each regression is the fixed per-invocation overhead — the cost the analyzer pays BEFORE processing any token (process spawn + catalog load for time; empty JSON skeleton for space). This is NOT a property of the algorithm itself; it is a property of the host system, the runtime, and the deployment shape. Re-running this study on a different machine, in a different language, or with a different launcher (containerized vs bare, cold-start vs warm) will produce a DIFFERENT intercept while the slope (the actual algorithmic O(n) work per token) stays essentially the same. The slope is what the panel should read as "the algorithm." The intercept is "the environment."

column1: ## F1 — overall + per-pattern (v4 run × pattern grain; per-pattern total = total runs)

column12: Overall F1 0.870 across 150 runs × 10 patterns. TP=171 are analyzer detections the cohort accepted; FP=19 are rare survey rejections; FN=32 are patterns participants ticked as "intended but missed"; TN=1278 dominates because each run only writes 1-2 patterns out of 10. Algorithm reliability shows in the small FP and FN counts.

high-familiarity creational pattern: 17 of 150 runs intended factory; analyzer caught 16 (recall 0.94). FP=1 is rare survey-rejection of correct detections; FN=1 is participants ticking "intended but missed" via the checkbox. TN=132 reflects runs where factory simply wasn't written and the analyzer correctly stayed silent.
